topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my quiet evening. My stomach clenched, a familiar, cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. I clicked the link, my heart hammering against my sternum like a trapped bird. The article, my meticulously researched piece, was live. But the byline… it belonged to a stranger. A name I didn't recognize, a face I hadn't seen. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge to steady myself. I stared at the text, the words that were undeniably mine, now attributed to someone else’s ambition. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, a profound sense of being adrift without my own anchor. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to retreat, to hide under the covers, anything to escape the exposure, the feeling of having nothing substantial of my own to stand on.

[story 2]

When I saw the publication date, the world seemed to tilt. It was mine. My voice, my arguments, the very structure of my prose—all there, yet draped in another person’s signature. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I picked up my phone, my fingers clumsy as they navigated to the original submission portal. The sheer impossibility of the situation made my vision blur momentarily. A tightness constricted my throat, making it hard to draw a full breath. I started pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards seeming too loud, too exposed. Every glance at the screen brought a fresh surge of inadequacy, a sinking feeling that my efforts, my very voice, were merely borrowed currency, easily taken away. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears.

[story 3]

The email arrived at precisely 2:17 AM. It was brief, professional, and utterly devastating. The headline screamed my name, but the author credit was undeniably incorrect. My initial reaction wasn't anger; it was a strange, paralyzing stillness. I reread the text, tracing the lines of my own carefully constructed sentences with a finger. A slow, creeping cold spread through my limbs. I felt a profound sense of uselessness, as if the energy I had poured into this work had simply evaporated, leaving me stranded. I stood up, walking to the window, looking out at the indifferent city lights. The weight of the situation pressed down, heavy and suffocating. I felt a desperate need to reach out, to call someone—anyone—to validate that this was real, that I hadn't imagined the theft of my creation. The silence from the phone was deafening, confirming the isolation.
